author: @sheerpride
text: Thanks for a great video. As you suggest, there isn’t one solution to the problem, but having a selection of alternatives will go a long way to alleviating the issues if we have blackouts. One option, for those with a car and parking next to their home is an inverter and an extension lead. You need to buy one with enough power to meet the electrical load, but an 800 watt inverter (connected directly to the 12v battery, NOT the aux power outlet) is going to run your fridge/freezer, USB/laptop charger, broadband hub and a lot of LED lights. You might even add a small tv, although this will reduce the time before the battery runs flat (or you start up the car to charge the battery). It will not power your kettle/electric hob, but that’s when your rocket stove fills the gap.

author: @salaciouscreations4323
text: I used your channel to make a chandle heater.   Not the plant pot stuff.   I got a massive pot candle 1kg of wax.  Melted it all out and replaced the wicks with lollypop sticks.  Lined all the pit with thick foil. And make a shroud out if an foil roasting tray.  Used tent pegs to hold up the shroud  and secured with electrical connectors.  Added a oven thermometer so I can check the temp inside.  Got a cheap stove fan that is powered by heat.   It pumps out 150°c - 200°c  heat inside the shroud  and the fans sucking out a steady stream of heat over 50°c.   Costs £2.99 for a wax refil from cheap candles and each fill lasts a week.   I use them as I made a few for each room.  And it's been over 25°c in each room.  I got smoke and co alarms in each of the rooms.   So thank you for your channel.  We are set for winter and I have 6 of the units now.  And enough wax to last until April.   If I can get the rooms to 18°c when we finally hit the minus temps I will be happy.   I am trying the meth heater next as I need to solve drying the washing. But I am totally ready if they do cut the power.  All thanks to you

text: Just to pick up on something you said. The UK doesn't buy 60% of its electricity from Europe. Its between 5-10% through interconnectors and about 4-12% for gas.  The problem is that about 50% of the electricity you have coming into your house is from a Gas Fired Power Station  The gas (like electricity)  is traded as a commodity,  the wholesale price dictates the cost of the gas used by the Gas Fired Power Station to generate electricity,  which is just passed onto the customers. Under the CEGB the UK was self sufficient but not anymore.

author: @TheEulerID
text:
Britain does not buy 60% of its electricity from Europe, or anything remotely lik it. The actual figures are 17 TWh with local generation of 318 TW, which means it is about 5%. Also, the dominant part of the imports is French nuclear power, not gas powered generation.

There are, of course, gas imports from Europe, but they are primarily from Norway which accounts for 77% of all UK gas imports, the vast majority of the rest being via LNG in one of the three re-gasification plants (which make up 20% of European capacity of that sort). author: @glumpy10
text:
Here in Oz there are going to be blackouts because of our insane zero Emissions fixation. Power stations are literally being blown up without any meaningful replacement of that generation. We are BS'ed to about batteries being built but that's storage, NOT generation. They also can supply about 2 Hours of power at BEST.  That's of course if the weather has been good and they are charged up. Hasn't stopped raining most of the year on the populated east coast so that is a fly in the ointment of this whole charade as well. 

  We are being deceived and going to be put in a lot of trouble under the guise of Climate derange but that is just an excuse for a larger money making scheme for big Biz. 

Last winter I built my own waste oil fired house heater.  The burner sits outside and the heat is transferred by water to another radiator which air is drawn through to go into the house. This eliminates all chance of the house being burned or flooded. 
The setup is capeable of far more heat than could ever be needed and of course costs nothing to run other than a few hundred watts of electricity which is made up for by the solar I have.  I have 30 Kw of solar but being an otherwise all electric house, there is no way the solar can meet our needs when we need it for heating. 

I Have built a couple of generators and plan on a couple more.  Not sure what I exactly which way I want to go with them yet so no harm in having backup and I like playing with them anyway.  They will all be run by Diesel engines also running on waste oil which I have been doing and with Vehicles for 25 Years last month. 
 Each 1000L IBC of oil I put away is a virtual 10, 000 KWH battery because that is the energy the oil contains. 

There is a fine line between being paranoid and being prepared.  My neighbour is a high level manager for a power co and says things are going to get bad and is also building his own power system.  If it turns out all is fine, I'll be very happy because the ramifications of outages are quite severe and not having lights on at home is pretty well down the list of problems in the grand scheme of things.  
If people call me paranoid,  not concerned. I was ridiculed for years running waste veg oil in my vehicles till the price of fuel went up now the same people that laughed at me are admitting, I am the one having the last laugh well and truly. 

If things do go for  the worst, then I will have had foresight to prepare and people will be wishing they had some self sufficiency as well.  The minute blackouts come, there won't be any generators left in the shops the same as when the fires came few years back every one sold out of fire pumps in 3 Days flat.  Of course Fuel may also be hard to get to runn a genny when there is no power at the pumps or to run EFPOS machines.  This has happened in California before and is a lesson worth remembering. 

Time you realise the problem, it's too late to do anything about it so you can only do it in advance. 
If I use my gennys or not, they are worth it to me because I feel secure I have them and they let me sleep real good, potentially
 warm or cool if I ever need them.

author: @gordonlawrence1448
text: As far as entertainment is concerned people get a computer which is way over-specified. Few of them take less than 400W when just streaming. Being a design engineer in the electronics industry I was not happy that just an ordinary MMS (Multi-Media System) would suck that amount of juice. I decided to see if there was a lower power option available. A J5040 motherboard from ASROCK was the order of the day as it only needs 10W going flat out. Low power DDR4 shaved off another couple of watts, for storage you need to go SATA as 1 an M.2 hard drive would be wasted in this system, and 2 would take far more power. IE SATA drives as low as 4W active ad 0.4W standby are available. I cheaped out on this and went for 5W active and 1W standby. A low power LCD screen was out of my price range so I got a regular one and the same goes for keyboard and mouse. Running flat out it only needs 40W (including screen and input devices) and is nice and quiet due to passive cooling. The other thing that surprises most people is how much the operating system affects power usage. Specifically Windows 10 has so much junk running in the background it can at worst case be using half your CPU bandwidth and that increases power usage considerably. Linux can be made to look and act like windows and of course Libre office is free. The whole lot in a very nice case cost less than £500 and if you already have a screen keyboard and mouse it's less than £350.

author: @trading7000
text: I am currently testing out a lighting/heating option. I took an empty Nescafe Coffee Jar and melted 2.5 blocks of TREX vegetable shortening in a pan, poured it into the Jar, I left the jar on the windowsill to re-solidify for a few hours (about 6 I think) and then pushed a cheap candle in the middle all the way down to the bottom of the jar. The candle stuck out of the solidified shortening a bit (but not much - about half an inch) and then I lit it at 6am last Saturday. It has been burning continuously now for 39 hours and I would estimate it has burnt down about 60 percent of the way down - this means it should last approximately 60 hours total before I need to redo it again. I managed to get the TREX for £1.13 each (on offer) and the candles were a couple of pounds for 8, so the whole cost is just over £3 for 5 days light/heat. I would recommend everyone does it and you'll have at least a cheap light source at the very least for not much

author: @debbiehenri345
text:
If you simply keep your freezer shut during those 3 hours of blackouts, that should be enough in itself. When we have one of our not infrequent power cuts here, we drape coats and blankets over  the lid and the sides (never over the mechanics at the back) to insulate it.
Also, if you need to replace your old freezer shortly - get a chest freezer and not one of these upright ones. Yes, they take up a little more room, but when you open the door to the chest cabinet, the cold stays within and doesn't flood out all over the floor as it does with an upright type. 

If you have a dozy teen that stands in front of the fridge with the door open for 20 minutes at a time (son used to do this), tell them to stop it or you'll quit buying them all the refrigerated items they like best. 

Buy long life milk instead of fresh, because at least you can store your stock of unopened cartons in the cupboard and just refrigerate one opened one at a time. Also, remember supermarkets will suffer blackouts too, so you won't know if the 'fresh' milk in their cabinets is on the turn. In fact, come to think of it, these blackouts could end up wasting a hell of a lot of food in shops. 

Candles - check. 
Wind-up lanterns/torches - check. 
Board games - check. 

Plenty of us around the UK have had power outages for days at a time during bad weather and, honestly, we should all be set up to survive them by now. 
We used to have a diesel generator to take care of a few lights, run the freezer, etc - and if we went outside of a night, our home was the only one showing any sign of normality. Time and again, we'd have power cuts lasting hours, even days, but neighbours wouldn't prepare themselves for the next, and I doubt they're better prepared now (even though they've had months of warnings). So, it proves that most people would rather get in a flap, worry about it, and then sit in the dark. Victims.

author: @martinsaint9999
text: The last five winters I already have shut off my refrigerator for six months because the temperature of my kitchen was already quite low and I did not need to cool the food. This saved quite a bit of electricity. I washed my laundry for a week by hand when my washing machine went kaput - so I know that even this can be done for a while (I used cold water and it worked). We have a well in our village where you can get water for washing. I also spend 100 Euros to buy a thick sleeping bag. It is very good for sleeping in 10 ° C cold rooms. My house was not heated for four months during the last winter, so I know it cools down to 7 °Celsius when the temperature on the outside is -4 ° C (I shut the water off and emtied the tubes). I am interested in buying clothing with integrated heating but have not bought any so far. I write this sitting in my unheated living room (13 ° C) under a blanket. 🙂

author: @WG1807
text:
Not for everyone to make but it seems like you might manage one.  That is, a Rocket Stove with Heat Exchanger vessel and then final flue (steel pipe) to the outdoors.  You can get the vessel to 500 degrees C (or less for safety) and use about 1/4 - 1/3 of the wood fuel you might use in a conventional wood burner.  It prefers sticks, twigs, pallet scraps and any little offcuts.  Logs will work but can be split far more than conventionally.

This is an indoor heater of high efficiency and huge output.  It's not a Rocket Stove Mass Heater that you may have seen (on the web) but a tweak on the same idea.

author: @davidsoulsby1102
text:
First I will say I agree with the idea of doing stuff for yourself and being as self sufficient is a good thing and should be encourage, I never liked the idea of the global market etc.
Secondly the chances of blackouts is tiny. I worked in the industry all my life and I can say its the most conservative industry you will ever get. Because the demand is so erratic over the day and the year the actual network is over engineered to a point where it can double the demand and cope nicely.
That doesn't cover the amount of energy you say, true but because of the erratic demand there has to be the ability to produce the maximum for times of maximum demand. That is where the import of energy comes in. The import covers the high demand peaks, it also covers the ecological need for green energy, our own surplus energy production is often coal and gas fired, so won't be used unless desperation point.
This all means if people are sensible and turn off unneeded lights, use shorter wash cycles etc there will be more than enough capacity. If the EU countries try to limit imports significantly they will have to break contracts and national agreements and that is unlikely as it would impact their credibility.
so a 10% drop is what should be expected and is fair as they need our assistance. We can quite computably absorb that with minimum effort.
Regarding the planning.
Every 2 years every company involved re-evaluates its emergency plans. This is mostly for storms etc but it also covers energy crisis's, every for years they test them out with exercises.
There are plans for terrorism, war, everything and constantly renewed.
The press and anti government groups like to catastrophize so the government looks bad as seen through Brexit and Covid. Thats what is happening at the moment. Just like the Chinese shown here.
I can't see the future obviously and situations change but I see no chance of blackouts as things stand, I still have some solar and batteries because of cost and local faults. but not scared of national blackouts.
The industry tend not to shout about plans and disasters as it doesn't look good for share holders....
Cheers

author: @williambianchi2006
text:
For emergency food, buy extra shelf stable foods you regularly eat. Dry goods like spaghetti and canned goods like tuna are good candidates. Want to up your game and have fresh veggies? Purchase seeds in bulk and learn how to sprout and grow microgreens. Harvest time is 2-4 days for sprouts and 1-2 weeks for microgreens, depending on what you're growing inside your home. High nutrition, seeds store very well, can grow inside during the winter, and very short harvest times for the win.
Water---Store extra drinking water. Get or make a water filter so you can make questionable water drinkable. Set up a distiller so you can distil water with your rocket stove. Set up a rainwater catch, as well.
Heat and cooking---Can't go wrong with a rocket stove. Burn wood if you have it or waste motor oil or even paper logs. I am going to try making a sand battery or two with a metal bucket/buckets of sand on the rocket stove and bring it inside to radiate the stored heat. A good zero-degree sleeping bag and hot water bottles should also help. A Sterno stove can even be used to cook, along with many heaters Robert showed us how to build.
Coffee---If you boil a kettle of water, you can very slowly pour that water over the coffee in your electric coffee maker a little at a time to make a pot of coffee without electricity. Bonus if you have a thermos to keep it hot after it's done "brewing." (Yes, I know I just listed coffee as a necessity. I did it because it is 😃)
 Light---Robert showed several setups to make a lantern. Can also purchase LED rechargeable lanterns and use solar to recharge the batteries. Shake flashlights, rechargeable flashlights, those flashlights that work when you squeeze to spin the little generator inside. Or, go to bed when the sun goes down and get up at sunrise.
Charging batteries to operate electric appliances---solar, wind, human power. Robert covers this, as well.
Protection/defense---follow the laws in your country and get creative if your options are limited. Hint, look in your toolbox and think about ways those tools could be employed, if necessary.
Best of luck.

author: @EmilNicolaiePerhinschi
text:
You can also reduce heat requirements by isolating the walls, and the cheapest way to do it that does not require permits is to hang carpets/blankets on the external walls, on the inside of the house. Also tape the joints on the windows you don't need to open.

UK houses rarely have double glazing so you can gain a bit of insulation by taping a transparent plastic sheet over the windows on the inside, trapping some air in between the windows and the sheet. 

The rocket stove will need wood :) . Furniture and fences make for poor burning wood because of all the treatments. 

This winter will very likely be mild since the solar cycle just started last year, but look for harder winters in 3-4 years.

All excellent points (as usual), but I have to take you to task on one item.  Y2K was a very big deal.  Nerds and geeks worked for a couple of years making sure that it wouldn't be such a big deal.  We intentionally did not Y2K-proof one redundant Compaq logging computer in the radio/TV stations I worked for.  At midnight 1999, I kissed my lovely better 1/3 (I'm a big guy) standing next to a warmed up and running 300 kW genset.  I looked at the logging computer before we left that morning, and its clock was no longer running.  Most folks didn't see the effects of Y2K because of the millions of man-hours invested in making sure they didn't.

author: @robertphillips93
text:
Wonderfully sane outlook -- thanks! Aside from the thin veneer aspect of globalism, it has the unfortunate quality of strengthening authoritarian states just as much as democratic and rule of law societies. 

The ground level experience for people in each when faced with shortages of essentials is bound to be very different. In a relatively freer environment, sober planning and resource availability make short and medium term survival considerably easier -- but not automatic.

A decent battery with simple means of using and charging it can supply a minimal level of service for basic needs, even up to refrigeration and air conditioning if resources permit. Your alcohol heater is a fine example of relatively safe and cheap gear for standby or even regular use. (I'm trying to design a hybrid between that and the safety burner you've showcased.) Water and food storage are worth considering as well, since disruption can occur locally or regionally as well as nationally or globally.

Few of us take the time to consider beforehand what we should do when faced with such problems unexpectedly. One thing to realise first is that for the most likely emergencies other than fire or flood, sheltering at home where important resources are already at hand, is by far the most preferable option.

replies.author: @James-L-83
replies.text:
An important note is that tiny sharp grooves scraped into the length of a tube will also assist in wicking, it was developed in passive cooling heatpipes. 
If it is just carbon then you could use cut fabric strips like old jean or dryer lint. Wrap it securely in two layers of foil with a tiny hole for gases to escape and lay it in hot coals for 45min, experiment on it. You could also take strips and wrap them in thin wire before baking to get the needed shape. Another option to try would be to make sticks of damp paper rolled up tight and bake.
I made one soaked in borax and sanitizer but my tube was made from scrap aluminum can and had air holes so it was drawing air and losing heat which cut off the wax fuel supply. Without enough fuel the carbon will burn down faster.
Also if you can move the combustion (oxidation) surface to a porous ceramic mantle it could greatly improve longevity and potentially the focused infrared output.
Remember, carbon monoxide increases dramatically as the fire loses access to oxygen, catalytic burners can be lethal but the pilot burner is supposed to cut the fuel before that. Don't trust it, be safe.

For refrigeration i put a couple of 2 litre bottles with ca. 5% saltwater in the freezer. That water freezes at -5°C so the cold lasts a bit longer. It works for me in my off grid cabin, as i disconnect the fridge during night.
Edit: as some pointed out in the answers, the salt only lowers the freezing point. So it's not necessary at all it seems.
On the other hand, this only makes sense if you have surplus energy that you can't store at some point of the day. That happens to me because I store energy generated from solar panels.

I don't know the situation with the foxcon workers and neither do I know how old that video is. However, I do know that the whole social credit score story is nonsense as I worked with a number of Chinese researchers and they would always laugh when they saw those stories on the BBC website.

Oh and Europe cut its own noise off with the sanctions and that doesnt include the terrorist distruction of the part German own Nord stream pipelines. Therefore to suggest that Russia is restricting energy supplies to Europe is laughable.

replies.author: @ChucksSEADnDEAD
replies.text: We didn't cut off our nose. We got rid of a manipulative and unreliable supplier. Russia did restrict gas supply - they stopped NS1 under the riddiculous excuse that they had oil leaks.

replies.author: @MrDirkles
replies.text:
@ChucksSEADnDEAD actually the turbine was sent to Canada for repair and the"sanctions" prevented it being shipped back to Russia. 

Europe has thrived on an agreement to use Russian energy over the years and this allowed Germany to become one of the largest , if not the largest, exporter in the world. Without cheap energy Europe and the UK are finished. Unlike Russia and the US we are not self sufficient.

replies.author: @ChucksSEADnDEAD
replies.text:
@MrDirkles The turbine was delivered to the pumping station July 17th. Hence the oil leak excuse. Are you admitting that you are talking about a subject you haven't kept up with, or just lying to carry water for Russia?
Hundreds of thousands dead or wounded isn't thriving. Cheap goods that are paid for in blood are not actually cheap. And Germany used the Russian gas as a plan to gradually decarbonize as gas is cleaner than coal. Germany also gambled with the divestment from nuclear. Russia is not self-sufficient. Their oil and gas extraction depends on Western expertise. If they are self-sufficient, what was the turbine doing in Canada?

replies.author: @MrDirkles
replies.text:
@ChucksSEADnDEAD I don't know the full story behind the nord stream turbine repair but I know it was sent to Canada. This may be because they simply had the servicing contract. Neither me nor you or even the BBC ;) know the full facts. 

I can't really make any sense of the rest of your post.

replies.author: @ChucksSEADnDEAD
replies.text: @MrDirkles The post makes perfect sense. The point is that Russia can't even fix their own oil or gas infrastructure. It's the West that does everything.
